Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I've been doing electrical work for decades in Milan, and I've learned that the best way to handle vaccine discussions is the same way I approach a complicated wiring problem—methodically and without rushing to conclusions. When someone brings up COVID vaccines, I don't just dismiss them or get heated, even though I've got strong feelings about health matters. I take a step back, just like I do on a job site when something doesn't add up.

The key is being honest about what you know and what you don't. I'm not a doctor, and I'm not ashamed to say that. What I do know is that I got my vaccines, and I've seen enough people in my community get sick or recover to understand that this isn't some simple yes-or-no question. When my son asks me about boosters or when someone at the volunteer center starts questioning the whole thing, I listen first. I ask questions. I try to understand where they're coming from before I start talking.

I think the problem is that people—myself included sometimes—get defensive too quickly. We treat vaccine conversations like they're personal attacks when they're really just people trying to make sense of complicated medical information. I've found that staying calm and admitting uncertainty actually helps more than pretending to have all the answers. If someone I know is hesitant, I tell them what my experience has been, but I also respect that they need to make their own decision.

My Jewish upbringing taught me that community health is important, but also that we need to listen to each other with compassion. That balance matters. On the few times I'm on social media, I try not to get pulled into heated arguments about vaccines because those never change anyone's mind. Instead, I focus on real conversations with real people—at football matches, during climbing trips, or when volunteering. Those conversations, where you can actually hear someone's concerns and share your own experience, they're where real understanding happens. That's how you manage these discussions without destroying relationships or your own peace of mind.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 55-year-old Italian electrician with high conscientiousness (89) and extraversion (84), indicating he's organized, reliable, and socially confident. His low openness (23) suggests he prefers established facts and practical approaches over abstract theorizing. His moderate agreeableness (43) and high neuroticism (84) indicate he can be direct and has strong emotional responses, but his good mental health and volunteering activities show emotional regulation. His vocational education and practical occupation inform his preference for hands-on understanding. His Jewish faith emphasizes community responsibility. His infrequent social media use and active hobbies suggest he values real-world interactions. These elements combine to create someone who is direct but thoughtful, community-minded but not naive, and who values practical problem-solving over ideological debate.
response_parsed.tone: Direct and practical with underlying emotional intensity; grounded in personal experience; respectful but honest; uses concrete examples from work and daily life; balances conviction with humility about limitations of knowledge; warm toward community concerns but resistant to abstract arguments; measured and deliberate despite strong feelings.
